class_schedule.topic: The Science and Practice of Clinical Psychology
learning_objectives: Describe the scientist-practitioner model and its implications for clinical work
Trace the history of clinical psychology from Witmer to evidence-based practice
Compare clinical psychology with related professions (counseling, psychiatry, social work)

lecture_notes.introduction: Clinical psychology is the science and practice of assessing, diagnosing, and treating psychological disorders. The scientist-practitioner model requires clinicians to be both consumers and producers of research.
key_concepts: Scientist-practitioner (Boulder) model vs. practitioner-scholar (Vail) model
Evidence-based practice: integrating research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values
Empirically supported treatments (ESTs) and the APA Division 12 criteria
Distinction between efficacy (controlled trials) and effectiveness (real-world outcomes)
Licensure requirements and ethical obligations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a published RCT of CBT for depression: evaluate methodology, results, and clinical implications

review.value: Clinical decisions integrate best available research evidence with clinical expertise and patient preferences/values
Efficacy: does the treatment work under controlled conditions? Effectiveness: does it work in real-world clinical settings with diverse populations?

class_schedule.topic: Clinical Assessment: Interviewing and Psychological Testing
learning_objectives: Describe structured, semi-structured, and unstructured clinical interview formats
Explain the psychometric properties required of clinical assessment instruments
Compare objective (MMPI-3) and projective (Rorschach) personality assessment approaches

lecture_notes.introduction: Clinical assessment is the systematic evaluation of psychological functioning. Accurate assessment is the foundation of diagnosis, treatment planning, and outcome evaluation.
key_concepts: Clinical interview: rapport, mental status examination, diagnostic assessment
MMPI-3: validity scales, clinical scales, interpretation
Rorschach Inkblot Method: comprehensive system vs. R-PAS
Neuropsychological assessment: WAIS-IV, Trail Making, Wisconsin Card Sorting
Cultural considerations in assessment: test bias and norming issues
lecture_notes.demonstration: Practice administering and scoring a brief mental status examination using role-play

review.value: MMPI scales that detect response styles (faking good, faking bad, inconsistency); essential for interpreting clinical scales
Systematic assessment of appearance, behavior, mood, affect, thought process, thought content, cognition, insight, and judgment

learning_objectives: Compare CBT, psychodynamic, humanistic, and systemic therapy approaches
Describe the common factors model of psychotherapy
Explain the role of the therapeutic alliance in treatment outcomes

lecture_notes.introduction: How does psychotherapy work? Research reveals that both specific techniques and common relational factors contribute to therapeutic change.
key_concepts: CBT: cognitive restructuring and behavioral experiments
Psychodynamic therapy: transference, interpretation, corrective emotional experience
Person-centered therapy: empathy, unconditional positive regard, genuineness
Common factors: therapeutic alliance, empathy, expectations, feedback
Dodo bird verdict: 'Everyone has won and all must have prizes' -- equivalent outcomes across approaches
lecture_notes.demonstration: Watch therapy demonstration videos and identify specific techniques and common factors in each

review.value: The collaborative bond between therapist and client; consistently the strongest predictor of therapy outcomes across all orientations
Wampold: factors shared across therapies (alliance, empathy, expectations) account for more variance in outcomes than specific techniques

class_schedule.topic: CBT in Practice: Techniques and Case Formulation
learning_objectives: Describe specific CBT techniques: cognitive restructuring, behavioral activation, exposure
Apply the collaborative empiricism approach to clinical work
readings: Beck, Cognitive Behavior Therapy: Basics and Beyond, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: CBT is the most extensively researched psychotherapy approach. This session provides hands-on practice with core CBT techniques and case formulation.
key_concepts: CBT case formulation: precipitating event, core beliefs, intermediate beliefs, automatic thoughts, behaviors, emotions
Cognitive restructuring: identifying and evaluating automatic thoughts using Socratic questioning
Behavioral experiments: testing predictions derived from maladaptive beliefs
Behavioral activation: scheduling activities to counteract avoidance and depression
Exposure hierarchies for anxiety disorders
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live role-play: therapist uses Socratic questioning to help a client evaluate an automatic thought

review.value: Guided discovery through open-ended questions that help the client examine evidence for and against their automatic thoughts
A planned experiential activity to test a specific prediction derived from a maladaptive belief; more powerful than verbal disputation alone

class_schedule.topic: Psychotherapy Outcome Research and the Evidence Base
learning_objectives: Evaluate the methodology of psychotherapy outcome research
Interpret meta-analytic findings on psychotherapy effectiveness
Discuss the dissemination gap between research and practice
readings: Pomerantz, Ch. 16; Lambert, 'The efficacy and effectiveness of psychotherapy' (handbook chapter)

lecture_notes.introduction: Does therapy work? The answer from decades of outcome research is clearly yes, but understanding the nuances of this evidence base is essential for clinical decision-making.
key_concepts: Meta-analysis of psychotherapy outcomes: average effect size d = 0.80
Dose-response relationship: most change occurs in early sessions
Deterioration effects: 5-10% of clients get worse in therapy
Therapist effects: therapist variability accounts for more outcome variance than technique
Implementation science: bridging the research-practice gap
lecture_notes.demonstration: Critical appraisal exercise: evaluate a meta-analysis of psychotherapy for a specific disorder

review.value: Meta-analyses consistently show psychotherapy produces large effects (d ≈ 0.80); the average treated client is better off than 79% of untreated controls
Individual therapists vary substantially in effectiveness; therapist interpersonal skills may matter more than the specific treatment model used

class_schedule.topic: Ethical and Professional Issues in Clinical Practice
learning_objectives: Apply the APA Ethics Code to clinical dilemmas involving confidentiality, multiple relationships, and competence
Describe the duty to warn/protect and its legal basis (Tarasoff)
Discuss cultural competence and its role in ethical clinical practice
readings: Pomerantz, Ch. 3-4; APA Ethics Code (selected standards)

lecture_notes.introduction: Clinical psychologists face ethical dilemmas that require balancing competing obligations. Ethical practice is not just following rules but engaging in ongoing moral reasoning.
key_concepts: APA Ethics Code: beneficence, nonmaleficence, integrity, justice, respect for autonomy
Confidentiality and its limits: duty to warn, child abuse reporting, court orders
Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California: duty to protect identifiable third parties
Multiple relationships and boundary crossings vs. boundary violations
Cultural competence: awareness, knowledge, skills for working with diverse populations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethics case conference: small groups analyze three clinical dilemmas and present recommendations

review.value: When a client poses a serious threat to an identifiable third party, the therapist has a duty to protect that person, overriding confidentiality
Ethical obligation to understand how cultural factors influence assessment, diagnosis, and treatment; not a destination but an ongoing process

questions.question: A client presents with depression. Describe how a cognitive-behavioral therapist and a psychodynamic therapist would each conceptualize the problem differently, and identify one specific technique each would use.
questions.answer: CBT conceptualization: Depression is maintained by negative automatic thoughts arising from maladaptive core beliefs (e.g., 'I am worthless'), behavioral avoidance, and reduced positive reinforcement. Technique: cognitive restructuring using thought records to identify, evaluate, and modify distorted automatic thoughts. Psychodynamic conceptualization: Depression stems from unresolved loss, internalized anger toward a lost object, or repetition of early relational patterns. Unconscious conflicts and defenses maintain the depressive state. Technique: interpretation of transference -- the therapist identifies how the client's relational patterns in early life are being replayed in the therapeutic relationship.

rubric.key: Clear contrast between the two approaches

questions.question: Explain what the 'Dodo bird verdict' means in psychotherapy research. What evidence supports it, and what is the most common argument against it?
questions.answer: The Dodo bird verdict (from Alice in Wonderland: 'Everyone has won and all must have prizes') refers to the finding that different psychotherapy orientations produce roughly equivalent outcomes. Evidence: meta-analyses by Wampold and others show that bona fide therapies intended to be therapeutic produce similar effect sizes, and common factors (alliance, empathy) account for more outcome variance than specific techniques. The main argument against it: for certain specific disorders, some treatments clearly outperform others (e.g., exposure therapy is superior for phobias, CBT outperforms other approaches for OCD). The verdict may hold for general distress but not for all specific conditions.

rubric.key: Correctly defines the Dodo bird verdict
Presents the counterargument about specific disorders

questions.question: A client tells their therapist they plan to seriously harm their roommate tonight. Describe the therapist's ethical and legal obligations, citing the relevant legal precedent.
questions.answer: The therapist has a duty to protect under the Tarasoff v. Regents (1976) ruling. The obligation includes: (1) warn the identifiable intended victim, (2) notify law enforcement, (3) take other reasonable steps to protect (e.g., hospitalization if the client meets criteria). This duty overrides confidentiality when there is a serious, imminent threat to an identifiable person. The therapist should document the threat assessment, the actions taken, and the rationale. After the immediate crisis, the therapist should address the therapeutic relationship and process the disclosure.

rubric.key: Cites Tarasoff and duty to protect
Lists specific actions the therapist must take

questions.question: Explain the dose-response relationship in psychotherapy. How many sessions does the average client need to show reliable improvement, and what are the clinical implications?
questions.answer: The dose-response relationship in psychotherapy shows that therapeutic improvement follows a negatively accelerating curve: most change occurs in the early sessions, with diminishing returns over time. Research by Howard et al. found that approximately 50% of clients show reliable improvement by session 8, and 75% by session 26. Clinical implications: (1) brief therapies can be effective for many clients, (2) clients who have not improved by session 8-10 may need a change in approach, (3) routine outcome monitoring can identify clients who are not on track, allowing for adaptive treatment adjustments.

rubric.key: Correctly describes the negatively accelerating dose-response curve
Provides approximate session numbers for improvement rates
Discusses clinical implications including outcome monitoring

major_assignments.title: Integrative Clinical Case Formulation
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive clinical case formulation for a provided complex case. Include diagnostic assessment, theoretical conceptualization from two orientations, evidence-based treatment plan, and discussion of ethical considerations. The case will involve cultural factors requiring culturally responsive formulation.
objectives: Conduct a thorough diagnostic assessment with differential diagnosis
Develop case formulations from two theoretical orientations
Create an evidence-based treatment plan with session-by-session goals
Address cultural and ethical considerations in clinical work

criteria: Accurate DSM-5-TR diagnosis with supporting evidence
Identifies maintaining factors and treatment targets
Thorough diagnostic assessment with cultural considerations
Detailed evidence-based treatment plan with session structure

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of clinical concepts, assessment methods, and therapy approaches

clinical_skills_labs.description: Role-play exercises in interviewing, assessment, and therapy techniques

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering assessment and theoretical orientations

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for clinical skills labs. Students who miss more than 2 labs will receive a reduced lab grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. The case formulation will not be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Clinical skills labs involve partner and group exercises. Written case formulations must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Confidentiality of role-play content must be maintained.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to DSM-5-TR, university library databases, and clinical assessment demonstration materials.
